coredata.dc:title: Quick and low cost synthesis of sulphur doped carbon dots by simple acidic carbonization of sucrose for the detection of Fe3+ ions in highly acidic environment
coredata.dc:creator: Naik, Vaibhav M.
Gunjal, Dattatray B.
Gore, Anil H.
Pawar, Samadhan P.
Mahanwar, Sunanda T.
Anbhule, Prashant V.
Kolekar, Govind B.
coredata.dc:description: An easy, facile, low cost and quick synthesis of sulphur doped carbon dots (S-C-dots) was carried out by acidic carbonization of sucrose (carbohydrate) without using any sophisticated instrument. The resultant S-C-dots shows excellent photoluminescent behaviour in highly acidic media with a quantum yield of 5.77%. Moreover, the as synthesized S-C-dots can be used as a dual probe for selective detection of Fe3+ ions in highly acidic conditions by Fluorescence as well as UV–vis spectroscopy. Also, the residual waste generated during the synthesis of S-C-dots can be used as an activated carbon (AC) for the application in dye adsorption from aqueous solutions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fe3+ sensing
Simple acidic carbonization
Dual probe
Activated carbon

Fluorescence behaviour of S-C-dots at different pH
Preparation and characterization of activated carbon

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: EDX pattern of S-C-dots showing presence of carbon, oxygen and sulphur.
(a) FT-IR and (b) Raman spectra of S-C-dots.
UV–Vis absorption, PL excitation and emission spectra of S-C-dots.
PL intensity of S-C-dots at different pH. The inset shows the linear response of S-C-dots between pH 3 to 7.

Graph of PL intensity of S-C-dots (a) in presence of different metal ion solutions (b) in presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ (c) The linear relationship between relative FL intensity and concentration of Fe3+

Graph of absorbance of S-C-dots in presence of (d) different metal ion solutions (e) different concentrations of Fe3+ (f) The linear relationship between relative absorbance and concentration of Fe3+.
The PL decay plot of S-C-dots in the absence and presence Fe3+.
Absorbance of MO dye solution in presence and absence of AC.
Schematic illustration of quenching of fluorescence of S-C-dots by Fe3+ ions.

head.ce:title: Quick and low cost synthesis of sulphur doped carbon dots by simple acidic carbonization of sucrose for the detection of Fe3+ ions in highly acidic environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fluorescence Spectroscopy Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Shivaji University, Kolhapur 416004, MS, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Rajashri Chhatrapati Shahu College, Kolhapur 416005, MS, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An easy, facile, low cost and quick synthesis of sulphur doped carbon dots (S-C-dots) was carried out by acidic carbonization of sucrose (carbohydrate) without using any sophisticated instrument. The resultant S-C-dots shows excellent photoluminescent behaviour in highly acidic media with a quantum yield of 5.77%. Moreover, the as synthesized S-C-dots can be used as a dual probe for selective detection of Fe3+ ions in highly acidic conditions by Fluorescence as well as UV–vis spectroscopy. Also, the residual waste generated during the synthesis of S-C-dots can be used as an activated carbon (AC) for the application in dye adsorption from aqueous solutions.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanomaterials (CNM's) have a unique place in nanoscience owing to their optical [1,2], electronic [2,4], and biocompatible properties [5,6]. Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles or carbon dots are a new class of carbon nanomaterials that have emerged recently which can act as potential competitors to conventional semiconductor quantum dots [1]. The synthesis of carbon dots is simple, cost effective and environment friendly which avoids the use of toxic materials and complex reaction conditions. Besides that, nature provides us countless sources for the synthesis of new carbon nanomaterials with splendid properties. Carbon dots show promising applications in the fields of chemical sensing [1,7], bio-sensing [6], bio-imaging [8], photocatalysis [2] and electrocatalysis [9,10] due to their comparable optical properties, low toxicity, environmental friendliness, low cost and simple synthetic routes [1–3]. Furthermore easy surface passivation, functionalization [11] and doping of carbon dots with heteroatoms such as sulphur, nitrogen and phosphorus [12,13] allow for the control of their physicochemical and optical properties.

In the area of sensing, it is very important to develop cost effective nanodevices with novel properties. Due to importance of metal ions fundamental role in a wide range of biological processes and aquatic environment, the development of metal ion sensors with high sensitivity and selectivity is necessary. A good sensor should have the ability to sense with least possible error irrespective of the external environment in which it is placed.

In this work we focus on the development of sulphur doped carbon dots (S-C-dots) as a fluorescent as well as UV–Vis spectrophotometric probe for the sensing of Fe3+ ions in highly acidic environment and application in pH sensing. Along with that, we additionally report the concurrent preparation of activated carbon as an adsorbent material for the adsorption of dye in aqueous solutions. The proposed method is simple, cost effective and environmental friendly where residual waste that is activated carbon formed during the synthesis of S-C-dots can be utilized in the process of water purification. This strategy does not require any complex instruments for the synthesis of S-C-dots in brief timeframe.

Sucrose was purchased from Molychem, Mumbai. Boric acid, citric acid, trisodium orthophosphate, MgCl2, KCl, NaCl, AlCl3, FeCl3, FeSO4, NiCl2, Pb(NO3)2, CoCl2, CuCl2, BaCl2, CaCl2, quinine sulphate, sulphuric acid were received from s d fine-chem, Mumbai. All chemical were of analytical reagent (AR) grade and used as received without further purification. All the solutions were prepared in double distilled water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Concurrent synthesis of fluorescent S-C-dots and an activated carbon
ce:section.ce:para: In this proposed work; easy, quick and low cost synthesis of S-C-dots was achieved by simple acidic carbonization of sucrose. 5 mL concentrated H2SO4 was added to 5 g sucrose with uniform stirring until slurry like mixture was obtained. This mixture was kept in a hood undisturbed for 5–10 min till formation of porous carbon cake. Then the black porous carbon cake was crushed and stirred in 100 mL double distilled water which was filtered to get clear pale yellow S-C-dot solution. The obtained residue (activated carbon) was washed with surplus amount of water, dried in an oven and pulverized to fine powder.

Quantum yield of the as synthesized carbon dots was determined using Quinine sulfate as a standard by comparative method using the following equation:
QY S
=
QY R
×
m S
/
m R
×
η S
2
/
η R
2
where subscript ‘S’ and ‘R’ refers to sample (S-C-dot solution) and reference (Quinine sulfate) respectively, QY is quantum yield, ‘m’ is slope and ‘η’ is refractive index. Quinine sulfate was prepared in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution (QY is 0.54 at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm). Slope was calculated by plotting graph of absorbance versus fluorescence for five different concentrations of quinine sulfate and S-C-dot solution which was substituted in above equation for the determination of quantum yield.

Fluorescent spectra and ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of synthesized carbon dots were obtained using JASCO spectrofluorometer FP-8300 and SPECORD 210 plus analytic jena spectrometer respectively. The luminescence decay profile of carbon dots was studied by using a time correlated single photon counting technique (TCSPC). The morphological and structural study of S-C-dots was carried out by using transmission electron microscopy (FEG-TEM 300 kV), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Raman spectroscopy (BRUKER RFS 27), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence behaviour of S-C-dots at different pH
ce:section.ce:para: For study of fluorescence behaviour at different pH, as synthesized S-C-dot solution was used. In a typical procedure, the fixed volume of carbon dot solution and buffer solution of different pH was diluted to 10 mL. The corresponding PL spectrum of all the solutions having pH 1 to 11 was obtained upon excitation at 360 nm.

The metal ion sensing capacity was investigated for 11 different metal ions under identical conditions. 2 mL 100 ppm solution of each metal ion along with 1 mL as synthesized S-C-dot solution was diluted to 10 mL and allowed to react for 5 min at room temperature. The UV–Vis spectra and fluorescence spectra of all these solutions were obtained at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. To study the Fe3+ sensing capacity of S-C-dots, different concentrations of Fe3+ were diluted to fixed volume and analysed in above manner.

The residual carbon was washed with excess of distilled water, dried and pulverized to fine powder and used as it is without further treatment in order to check its applicability as an adsorbent material. 0.1 g AC powder was stirred in dye solution (methyl orange) and kept overnight. The absorbance of dye solution before and after addition of activated carbon was noted down to confirm the adsorption phenomena. Also the efficiency of the AC was measured by calculating surface area using BET technique.

Tenable mechanism for the formation of S-C-dots: Sucrose, a carbohydrate or in simple words hydrated carbon, when treated with concentrated H2SO4 which is a good dehydrating agent, results in the formation of carbon due to dehydration of sucrose [14]. The reaction is as follows:
C 12
H 2
O
11
→
Concentrated
H 2
SO 4
12 C + 11
H 2
O

The process involves formation of carbon in varying sizes. The division of small sized particles from bulk is accomplished by straightforward filtration. The dehydrating and oxidizing property of concentrated H2SO4 may be responsible for the formation and surface passivation of S-C-dots. Also, in the process of synthesis, temperature plays a vital role. Since response of sucrose with concentrated H2SO4 is an exothermic one, there is no requirement for external supply of heat energy. The heat generated in the response itself is adequate for the formation of S-C-dots. In addition to this, H2SO4 also acts as a doping agent where carbon dots are in-situ doped with sulphur atom during the process of synthesis [15].

The morphological and size distribution study of S-C-dots was carried out using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). TEM image of S-C-dots shows almost uniform spherical morphology having an average diameter of 2.3 nm as portrayed in Fig. 1 . The XRD pattern of S-C-dots, as depicted in Fig. 2 shows broad peak at 2θ ≈ 23.4° which can be assigned to highly disordered carbon atoms [11,15,16]. The peak beyond this position at 2θ ≈ 23.7° is ascribed to S atom according to JCPDS card number 00-001-0478. The intense peak at 2θ ≈ 26.5° is due to the formation of CS composite. The appeared doublet at the corresponding position is due to the occupation of interstitial position of carbon by sulphur atoms. Further, doping of sulphur in carbon dots was confirmed by EDX examination which demonstrates the presence of carbon, oxygen and sulphur in S-C-dots as delineated in Fig. 3 . The FT-IR spectrum of S-C-dots show different characteristic peaks (Fig. 4 ). The broad peak emerging at 3318 cm−1 is due to OH stretching vibrations since S-C-dots are dispersed in water [8,19]. The peaks at 2115 cm−1 and 1636 cm−1 are due to CC and CC respectively. The band at 1195 cm−1can be assigned to CS bond whereas band at 1051 cm−1 is consigned to CO bond. Based on the FT-IR survey, it can be concluded that plentiful of hydrophilic functional groups may be present on S-C-dot surface which shows excellent water solubility and aids in complex formation [14]. The Raman spectrum (Fig. 4b) of S-C-dots showed two peaks, D band at 1343 cm−1 (sp3 hybrid carbon) and G band at 1570 cm−1 (sp2 hybrid carbon). The D band is ascribed to the lattice defects and the G band corresponds to the graphitic nature of S-C-dots [15].

Iron is one of the most significant metals in biological systems as well as in an environmental view point. Therefore the detection of this metal ion is very important in biological systems and also an environmental and industrial pollution is concerned [20]. But majority of the metal contaminants in an aquatic system predominantly exist as suspended solids. Hence almost all metal investigation strategies execute usual acidification treatment of samples for precise assessment of their actual soluble metal content. Many Fe3+ sensitive fluorescent probes are available [14,16,21–23]. But very few reports claim about high fluorescent intensity of carbon dots at very low pH. Thus to overcome this problem we have focused on quick and easy synthesis of S-C-dots as a fluorescent and UV–Vis spectrophotometric dual probe for Fe3+ sensing in highly acidic media.

The fluorescence and absorbance of S-C-dots was investigated in presence of 11 different metal ions (Fig. 7a and d). However, the significant change in optical behaviour of S-C-dots was observed in presence of Fe3+ ions which resulted in quenching of fluorescence intensity (Scheme 1S) and enhancement in absorbance of S-C-dots. Such behaviour of S-C-dots indicates their sensing selectivity towards Fe3+ ions in presence of various other metal ions (Fig. 3S). In order to check the linearity of this system, absorbance and fluorescence of S-C-dots in presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ was investigated. It was found that as the concentration of Fe3+ ions increased, there was a linear diminution in fluorescence intensity (Fig. 7b) and enhancement in absorbance (Fig. 7e) of S-C-dots. The linear detection range of S-C-dots towards Fe3+ is from 0 to 872 μM with LOD 0.56 μM fluorometrically (Fig. 7c) and from 0 to 290 μM with LOD 32.30 nM by UV–Vis spectrophotometrically (Fig. 7f). The quenching mechanism of S-C-dots in presence of Fe3+ ions can be clarified with the assistance of PL decay study. As depicted in Fig. 8 , the average lifetime of S-C-dots in absence and presence of Fe3+ ions was calculated to be 1.12 ns and 1.19 ns respectively which suggest that there was no any significant change in the life time of S-C-dots before and after addition of Fe3+ ions. This clearly indicates that there is a formation of ground state complex between Fe3+ and S-C-dots, which resulted in the quenching of fluorescence intensity of S-C-dots (Scheme 1). To confirm the selectivity and reliability of this system, we explored the applicability of this method to tap and pond water system (Table 2S). A standard addition method [16] was performed in which water samples were filtered and then spiked with different concentrations of Fe3+ ion solution. In spite of the presence of many other minerals and metal ions in pond water system, there were no any specific effects in sensing behaviour of S-C-dots towards Fe3+ ions. Owing to good selectivity and dual probe nature towards Fe3+ sensing, the present sensor can be applied for detection of Fe3+ ions in real water sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of activated carbon
ce:section.ce:para: AC with excellent surface characteristics plays a significant role as a versatile adsorbent material [29–31]. AC is used in different areas such as waste water treatment [31–33], purification of drinking water [34], gas phase adsorption [35], liquid phase adsorption [36] and in supercapacitors [37,38].

The carbonization of sucrose (carbohydrate) was carried out with concentrated H2SO4 to give AC. This activated carbon powder was thoroughly washed with water to remove excess acid, dried in an oven and crushed to fine powder for surface area characterization. The surface area of the same was calculated as 6.035 m2/g having pore size 1.55 nm using BET technique. Based on surface area and pore size results; the above prepared AC can be conveniently used for various applications due to its adsorbing nature. In comparison with commercial activated carbon, the activity of as synthesized activated carbon is less. But it is competitive when it comes to cost effective method of synthesis as compared to other adsorbents. However with some special treatments, the activity of as synthesized carbon can be enhanced [35–38]. Here, AC was successfully used for Methyl Orange (MO) dye (30 ppm) adsorption in aqueous solution as shown in Fig. 9 .

In conclusion; simple, quick and low cost synthesis of S-C-dots was accomplished by acidic carbonization of sucrose (carbohydrate) without utilizing any sophisticated instrument. The prepared S-C-dots demonstrate an amazing luminescent behaviour in highly acidic media that can be utilized as a dual probe for selective determination of Fe3+ ions in real water samples. It was also observed that the synthesized S-C-dots have pH dependent emission showing linear range between pH 3 to 7. The residual waste formed during the synthesis of S-C-dots can be used as an adsorbent material for dye adsorption in aqueous solutions thus showing its applications in waste water treatment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge to the Department of Science and Technology (DST) New Delhi (India) for providing financial support (DST/INSPIRE Fellowship/2016/IF160441), and Shivaji University, Kolhapur (MS), India for providing facilities in our department.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2018.07.018.
